campaign: Aya Nakamura / Haut Niveau — Rising Above (The Exclusive Documentary)
one_liner: Lancôme answers racist attacks with Aya's own documentary.
search_doc:
Lancôme answers racist attacks with Aya's own documentary.
Mechanisms: ambassador-defence, documentary-as-campaign, cultural-icon-elevation, audience-precision-narrow, heritage-modernised
Idea: In light of the increasing consumer appetite for deeper emotional resonance and cultural relevance of luxury brands, it was French luxury leader Lancôme's priority to find new ways to meaningfully engage with a younger audience. This documentary aims to anchor Lancôme as a partner in pop culture and connect with Gen Z by portraying Aya in a new light, both in narrative and form.

full_record.file_path: 2024/LUXURY & LIFESTYLE/GOLD/Aya Nakamura _ Haut Niveau - Lancôme (Publicis Luxe Paris).jpg

extracted.campaign: Aya Nakamura / Haut Niveau — Rising Above (The Exclusive Documentary)
extracted.tagline: Haut Niveau / Rising Above — The Exclusive Documentary
extracted.context: Aya Nakamura is the most listened-to French artist worldwide with 6 billion total streams, 100 million YouTube views for her single «Djadja», telling out three Accord Arena shows in Paris within 15 minutes. When a man rumored that President Macron wanted her to sing at the Paris 2024 Olympics, she incited harsh criticism and racist insults from far-right factions, exposing the deep social and generational divisions in France.
extracted.idea: In light of the increasing consumer appetite for deeper emotional resonance and cultural relevance of luxury brands, it was French luxury leader Lancôme's priority to find new ways to meaningfully engage with a younger audience. This documentary aims to anchor Lancôme as a partner in pop culture and connect with Gen Z by portraying Aya in a new light, both in narrative and form.
execution: Title of the documentary 'Aya Nakamura: Haut Niveau (High Level)' encapsulates the essence of the creative idea: to portray Aya as an individual not just as a star, but as a person that rises above the noise.
Nakamura always remains her true self by painting a fuller, more authentic picture of her personal experiences, creativity and aspirations as an artist, mother and woman.

A new narrative for Aya & Lancôme — in spite of the controversy, it was crucial for Lancôme to show unequivocal support towards their Global Brand Ambassador. Reflecting the brand message that seeks to empower women to pave their own path, the goal of the documentary was to provide Nakamura the opportunity to reclaim her narrative, to tell her story with her own voice. In turn, the documentary also allowed Lancôme to craft new brand narratives that resonate deeper with its Gen Z audience.
Exclusive long-form documentary release
Cinematic photography campaign with Aya

inferred.one_liner: Lancôme answers racist attacks with Aya's own documentary.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Defended its Global Brand Ambassador Aya Nakamura against racist backlash with a cinematic 'Haut Niveau' documentary that reclaimed her narrative, drove 6.9M TikTok and 534K YouTube views, 95% positive sentiment and zero paid media spend.

visual.style_description: High-fashion editorial board led by a black-and-white sculptural portrait of Aya Nakamura crouching, eyes locked to camera, against a creamy backdrop; her name is set in an elegant serif typographic logo to the left, body copy in three columns of dense long-form text, with a row of cinematic stills and a small Lancôme rose-script logo across the bottom.

idea.rationale: Answering racist attacks against a global brand ambassador with a long-form ambassador-authored documentary — rather than a defensive press statement or a polished beauty film — is an unusually grown-up move for prestige beauty. The 'Haut Niveau' title turns the racist criticism back on itself as a portrait of an artist literally rising above it, which is a sharp, on-brand creative reframe.

strategy.rationale: Lancôme is L'Oréal Luxe's French heritage beauty house whose long-running 'Live Your Strength' mission empowers women to pave their own path, so standing publicly behind Aya Nakamura when far-right factions attacked her over the Paris 2024 Olympics rumour is exact mission-fit, not borrowed activism. The context is data-anchored — 6 billion total streams, 100M YouTube views for Djadja, three sold-out Accor Arena dates in 15 minutes — making the cultural-stakes case credible rather than rhetorical. The bet that a Gen Z luxury audience demands deeper cultural relevance from heritage brands is coherent with the ambassador relationship Lancôme already had in place.

execution.rationale: An exclusive long-form documentary plus a cinematic photography campaign is a tight, restrained orchestration that lets Aya reclaim her own narrative as artist, mother and woman, with TikTok teasers seeding a younger audience before the YouTube release. The choice of documentary-as-campaign — rather than the usual beauty product film — matches both the cultural weight of the controversy and the Gen Z appetite for ambassador authenticity, and the $0 paid-media claim implies organic seeding rather than amplification engineering.

impact.rationale: 6.9M combined TikTok teaser views and 534K YouTube views are entrant-reported and modest in absolute terms for a global beauty house, but the $0 media investment makes the earned-to-paid ratio the real story. The 95% positive sentiment figure is unanchored — '95% of how many measured comments?' is not disclosed — so it reads as a directional claim rather than a hard outcome. Skincare or fragrance sales lift, the obvious business outcome for Lancôme, is absent from the board, so impact rests on brand-equity signalling with a Gen Z audience rather than proven category-purchase intent.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Gold because Lancôme defended its ambassador through her own voice in a feature documentary — turning a racist attack into a mission-fit cultural statement that earned 6.9M views on zero paid media.

one_liner: The luxury brand becomes the shelf for other bottegas.
search_doc:
The luxury brand becomes the shelf for other bottegas.
Mechanisms: name-as-content, media-as-gift, craft-solidarity, shelf-share-handover, heritage-modernised
Idea: Bottega Veneta promoted smaller bottegas around the world by sharing the stage and its visibility with them, during the time when luxury brands usually triple their sales: the Holiday Season. The products from small bottegas appeared on the shelves of Bottega Veneta stores, took over Bottega Veneta murals and OOH worldwide and were promoted by Bottega Veneta influencers and celebrities.
Insight: The word 'bottega' belongs to every small artisan workshop on earth, not to a single luxury brand — sharing that name back is the most authentic way for Bottega Veneta to defend craft.

full_record.file_path: 2024/LUXURY & LIFESTYLE/SILVER/Bottega For bottegas - Bottega Veneta (LePub Milan).jpg

extracted.context: There's one word that represents excellence in craftsmanship in the world: bottega. After covid, 10,000 bottegas closed around the world. Now, because of the energy crisis, inflation and increase in cost of materials, thousand other bottegas are at risk.
extracted.idea: Bottega Veneta promoted smaller bottegas around the world by sharing the stage and its visibility with them, during the time when luxury brands usually triple their sales: the Holiday Season. The products from small bottegas appeared on the shelves of Bottega Veneta stores, took over Bottega Veneta murals and OOH worldwide and were promoted by Bottega Veneta influencers and celebrities.
execution: Products from small artisan bottegas placed on Bottega Veneta store shelves
Bottega Veneta murals and OOH worldwide handed over to small bottegas
Bottega Veneta influencers and celebrities promoting the artisan partners
Holiday Season activation across global retail
metrics: +79% sales in 2021 for all the small bottegas
press_coverage: Highsnobiety: "Bottega Veneta's latest project is all Bottega, no Veneta"
Elle Decor: "A small gesture for something bigger than us"
Hypebeast: "Bottega Veneta uses its global reach to shine a spotlight on the bottegas"

inferred.insight: The word 'bottega' belongs to every small artisan workshop on earth, not to a single luxury brand — sharing that name back is the most authentic way for Bottega Veneta to defend craft.
inferred.one_liner: The luxury brand becomes the shelf for other bottegas.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Reframed Bottega Veneta's name and global media as a platform for the world's surviving artisan bottegas during peak sales season; drove +94% brand visibility, +165% site visits and +79% sales for the partner workshops.

visual.style_description: Wide retail-shelf composition with a clean white Bottega Veneta store interior as the hero, on which artisan products from small bottegas (ceramics, wine bottles, soaps, leather goods) sit on the shelves while the campaign script wordmark 'Bottega for Bottegas' is overlaid in vivid acid green; press quote callouts flank the top corners and a bottom OOH-and-product photo strip anchors the layout.

idea.rationale: Surrendering the literal name of the luxury house back to the world's small artisan workshops that share it — and doing so during the Holiday Season when luxury triples its sales — is a sharp linguistic reframe and a real act of media generosity. The idea is structurally simple: the brand becomes the shelf for the bottegas it borrowed its name from, which is rare territory for prestige fashion in peak commercial weeks.

strategy.rationale: Bottega Veneta is Kering's Italian leather house whose mission has always been quiet craftsmanship over logos, so defending the survival of small artisan bottegas is exact mission-fit rather than borrowed purpose. The context is data-anchored — 10,000 bottegas closed after Covid, with thousands more at risk from the energy crisis, inflation and material costs — making the urgency real rather than rhetorical. The insight that the word 'bottega' belongs to every small workshop on earth, not to a single luxury brand, gives the maison standing to share rather than appropriate.

execution.rationale: Orchestration is a coherent take-over of Bottega Veneta's owned retail and paid OOH: artisan products from small bottegas are physically placed on the brand's store shelves, global murals and OOH are handed over to the partners, and the brand's influencer and celebrity roster amplifies the workshops by name during Holiday. The mechanic is right for the brief, though the board does not detail country coverage, number of partner bottegas or duration, leaving the rollout scale somewhat undefined for a global house.

impact.rationale: +94% brand visibility, +165% site visits and +79% sales 'in 2021' are presented as headline lifts but with no absolute baseline disclosed — '+79% of how many euros for which bottegas?' remains open — so the figures read as directional rather than auditable. Crucially the metrics measure the partner bottegas, not Bottega Veneta itself, so the brand's own business outcome during its highest-revenue season is conspicuously absent. Earned editorial across Highsnobiety, Hypebeast, Elle Decor and Bazaar is a credible signal of fashion-press resonance, with the Highsnobiety line 'all Bottega, no Veneta' capturing the cultural reframe.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because giving the brand's name and shelves back to the world's surviving artisan bottegas during Holiday is genuine mission-fit generosity, but the impact case rests on partner-side percentages without an absolute base.

one_liner: Fifty global luxury houses make pieces from Turkish heritage.
search_doc:
Fifty global luxury houses make pieces from Turkish heritage.
Mechanisms: heritage-modernised, artist-collab-luxury, cultural-icon-elevation, archive-as-campaign, materiality-as-the-point
Idea: Luxury has been a notion that has always been associated with the West. Beymen wanted to change the narrative and remind the world of the power of Turkish legacy over the world of fashion throughout history.
Insight: Turkish craftsmanship has secretly shaped global luxury for centuries — making the world's top maisons publicly credit it reclaims the heritage for Turkey.

extracted.tagline: Creating a timeless archive for Turkish legacy of luxury — The world's 50 greatest brands each created a piece inspired by luxury coming from Turkish lands.
extracted.context: Beymen has become the luxury destination of the world's most prominent designers for over 50 years. We embarked a comprehensive approach to celebrate the cultural legacy which have come out from these lands and have inspired the whole world for 300 years.
extracted.idea: Luxury has been a notion that has always been associated with the West. Beymen wanted to change the narrative and remind the world of the power of Turkish legacy over the world of fashion throughout history.
execution: World's greatest 50 brands (Dior, Valentino, Tom Ford, Balmain and others) invited to create a piece inspired by Turkish lands
Each brand designed an exclusive piece inspired by motifs of traditional Turkish culture

50 product designs unveiled (Valentino tulip-print bag, Off-White Ottoman-print jacket, Christian Louboutin Turkish chesmeran shoes, Dior gold-leafing kaftan, Alexander McQueen Turkish fez, Balmain hero ensemble)
Golden Opulence exhibition in Istanbul
Communication via global and local media

inferred.insight: Turkish craftsmanship has secretly shaped global luxury for centuries — making the world's top maisons publicly credit it reclaims the heritage for Turkey.
inferred.one_liner: Fifty global luxury houses make pieces from Turkish heritage.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Commissioned 50 of the world's leading luxury maisons to design exclusive pieces inspired by Turkish heritage, anchoring an Istanbul exhibition that recast Beymen as the curator of a timeless Turkish luxury archive and earned editorial pickup in Bazaar and Vogue.

visual.style_description: Dark editorial poster centred on a richly embroidered Ottoman-style golden Balmain ensemble as the hero figure, surrounded by floating thumbnails of pieces from Valentino, Off-White, Golden Goose, Christian Louboutin, Dior, Alexander McQueen and others; large serif 'Golden Opulence' title in cream over deep black, with text columns running along both edges.

idea.rationale: Inviting fifty of the world's leading luxury maisons to each design a piece inspired by Turkish heritage — and curating them into a single archive — is an ambitious narrative inversion: the West's prestige houses publicly source from Turkish craft rather than the other way around. The execution-level pieces (Valentino tulip-print bag, Dior gold-leafing kaftan, Louboutin chesmeran shoes, McQueen fez) make the reframe tangible, though the underlying mechanic — commissioned brand collaborations curated under a house theme — is a familiar luxury format.

strategy.rationale: Beymen is Turkey's leading luxury multi-brand retailer, founded 1971 in Istanbul and positioned as the country's destination for global luxury fashion, so curating an archive that reclaims Turkish heritage for the global maisons it stocks is a coherent brand-platform move. The 300-year cultural-influence claim is asserted rather than evidenced on the board (no specific motifs, periods or design-history references are cited as proof), so the heritage argument reads as house statement rather than data-anchored insight. As a retailer-led platform, mission-fit is real but narrower than a heritage maison defending its own craft.

execution.rationale: Orchestration produced 50 commissioned pieces from named maisons (Dior, Valentino, Tom Ford, Balmain, Off-White, Louboutin, McQueen and others), unveiled through a Golden Opulence exhibition in Istanbul and supported by global and local media. The artefacts themselves are the medium and they are credibly luxury-grade, but the board describes the rollout in broad strokes only — no phased calendar, no retail integration, no digital extension, no partnership programme is detailed. For a 50-brand global commission, the orchestration evidence on the board is thinner than the ambition.

impact.rationale: The board reports no metrics — no exhibition footfall, no sales lift, no earned-media value, no brand-tracking shift — so the business outcome a luxury retailer cares about (category share, basket size, brand-equity movement) is conspicuously absent. Press coverage is limited to two named outlets (Bazaar, Vogue), which is credible directional signal of fashion-press validation but very thin evidence base for a campaign of this scale. The cultural-reclamation ambition is real but the impact case as presented is essentially unproven.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the 50-maison Turkish-heritage commission is an ambitious cultural-reclamation platform, but the board carries no metrics and only two named press outlets — the impact case is largely unproven.

one_liner: The bangle grips the hoof, the brooch becomes a cigarette.
search_doc:
The bangle grips the hoof, the brooch becomes a cigarette.
Mechanisms: restraint-as-craft, cultural-icon-elevation, category-codes-subversion, materiality-as-the-point, fashion-photography-as-platform
Idea: Cavalier jewellery sounds like an oxymoron… A luxury Maison shouldn't be casual. This campaign deliberately twists the codes of high jewellery: a precious brooch is casually tucked behind the ear like a cigarette, or a hand wearing an iconic Hermès bangle grips the powerful, dark hoof of a horse. A reappropriation of the object in one instance, and a bold parallel in another: from one season to the next, Hermès allows itself unusual gestures in a highly codified world, to stage unexpected, impertinent associations. It is this cavalier attitude that embodies the spirit of the Maison — always elegant but never taking itself too seriously — and paints a unique picture of the Hermès woman.
Insight: In the brief, Hermès entrusted us with the mission of reinstating jewellery as quintessential Hermès objects, mirroring our approach with Hermès Beauty campaigns.

full_record.file_path: 2024/LUXURY & LIFESTYLE/BRONZE/Hermès, Cavalier Jewellery - Hermes (Publicis et Nous, Paris).webp

extracted.context: From horses to jewellery, there is but a stride. Métier has always held a place of prominence alongside leather. It serves as the link facilitating dialogue and unhindered movement. Jewellery perpetuates this freedom of movement. As jewellers of shapes, Hermès reinterprets its rules by surpassing the language of forms — the design of harnesses and stirrups to create personal, unique collections.
extracted.insight: In the brief, Hermès entrusted us with the mission of reinstating jewellery as quintessential Hermès objects, mirroring our approach with Hermès Beauty campaigns.
extracted.idea: Cavalier jewellery sounds like an oxymoron… A luxury Maison shouldn't be casual. This campaign deliberately twists the codes of high jewellery: a precious brooch is casually tucked behind the ear like a cigarette, or a hand wearing an iconic Hermès bangle grips the powerful, dark hoof of a horse. A reappropriation of the object in one instance, and a bold parallel in another: from one season to the next, Hermès allows itself unusual gestures in a highly codified world, to stage unexpected, impertinent associations. It is this cavalier attitude that embodies the spirit of the Maison — always elegant but never taking itself too seriously — and paints a unique picture of the Hermès woman.
execution: Print campaign with a model's bangled hand gripping a horse's hoof
Companion frame: brooch tucked behind a model's ear like a cigarette
Series of fashion photography spreads by Craig McDean and Richard Burbridge
Edge-perspective campaign extending across Hermès jewellery
press_coverage: 'Complices' editorial collaboration with Marie-Amélie Sauvé and Craig McDean / Richard Burbridge cited as part of the campaign

inferred.one_liner: The bangle grips the hoof, the brooch becomes a cigarette.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Reasserted Hermès jewellery as quintessential Hermès by deliberately mis-using it — bangle on a horse's hoof, brooch tucked behind an ear — restaging equestrian codes in a McDean / Burbridge editorial that re-positioned the category around Hermès' tongue-in-cheek elegance.

visual.style_description: Two-image fashion editorial pairing: left, a tight cropped photograph of a slim hand wearing a chunky chrome Hermès bracelet gripping the dark hoof of a horse; right, a high-contrast black-and-white portrait of a model with a precious brooch tucked behind the ear; centred Hermès Paris logotype and 'Cavalier jewellery' wordmark, four short text blocks in restrained serif sit below.

idea.rationale: Deliberately mis-using Hermès jewellery — a bangled hand gripping a horse's hoof, a brooch tucked behind an ear like a cigarette — to dismantle the over-codified language of high jewellery is a restrained, on-brand subversion. The two images carry the campaign's whole conceptual load through composition rather than copy, which is rare in jewellery advertising and faithful to Hermès' tongue-in-cheek elegance.

strategy.rationale: Hermès is the family-controlled Parisian Maison founded 1837 as a saddle-maker, whose entire identity rests on equestrian heritage extending into leather, silk and jewellery, so reinterpreting jewellery through harness and stirrup language is exact mission-fit rather than borrowed metaphor. The strategic brief — reinstating jewellery as quintessential Hermès objects, mirroring the approach already taken with Hermès Beauty — is internally coherent and explicit on the board. The 'cavalier' double-meaning (horseman / impertinent) is a precise French wordplay that anchors the campaign in Hermès' specific cultural vocabulary.

execution.rationale: Orchestration is deliberately narrow: a print campaign of two paired editorial images shot by Craig McDean and Richard Burbridge with Marie-Amélie Sauvé under the 'Complices' editorial collaboration, with an edge-perspective treatment extending across the jewellery line. The craft is high — the photography, framing and restraint match top-tier fashion editorial — but the channel mix as described is single-discipline (print / fashion editorial), with no digital, retail, film or activation layers detailed on the board.

impact.rationale: The board reports no metrics at all — no jewellery category sales lift, no editorial reach figures, no brand-tracking movement, no earned-media value — so the business outcome a luxury house typically demonstrates is entirely absent. Press coverage is limited to citing the 'Complices' editorial collaboration as part of the campaign rather than naming independent media validation. The impact case as presented rests almost entirely on craft assertion, with no quantitative or earned-media evidence to anchor it.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the cavalier mis-use of jewellery is a precise, on-mission Hermès subversion at the image-craft level, but the board carries no metrics and no independent press — the impact case is undocumented.

campaign: The Tale of Loewe x Suna Fujita
one_liner: Every campaign touchpoint, handmade like the ceramics.
search_doc:
Every campaign touchpoint, handmade like the ceramics.
Mechanisms: artist-collab-luxury, craft-as-the-medium, needle-felted-storytelling, world-building-extension, materiality-as-the-point
Idea: Luxury fashion house Loewe teamed up with an undiscovered ceramicist studio from a husband-and-wife team in Kyoto, whose pieces draw inspiration from their life, son, pet dog, and the idyllic countryside in which they live. Together, they turned five key ceramics into a collection of clothes and accessories, backed by a campaign with the same level of attention to detail and craftsmanship as the pieces themselves.
Insight: True luxury craft is best honoured by matching the partner's level of handmade detail at every touchpoint of the campaign, not just on the product.

full_record.file_path: 2024/LUXURY & LIFESTYLE/GRAND PRIX/Loewe X Suna Fujita  - Loewe (Loewe Madrid).jpg

extracted.campaign: The Tale of Loewe x Suna Fujita
extracted.tagline: How a luxury fashion house honoured the craft of undiscovered Japanese artists with an enchanting campaign for a show-stopping collection.
extracted.idea: Luxury fashion house Loewe teamed up with an undiscovered ceramicist studio from a husband-and-wife team in Kyoto, whose pieces draw inspiration from their life, son, pet dog, and the idyllic countryside in which they live. Together, they turned five key ceramics into a collection of clothes and accessories, backed by a campaign with the same level of attention to detail and craftsmanship as the pieces themselves.
execution: From five key ceramics to a key campaign with craft as every touchpoint
Animated films — hand-crafted characters in needle-felted wool
Documentary — dedicated films to get to know the ceramic couple Suna Fujita
Packaging — crafted packaging were more landscapes for the characters
Activations — pop-ups including a candy-store-immersed people in the ceramicists' world
OOH — 3D digital billboards where characters sprung to life
Book — a custom-made children's pop-up book

inferred.context: Luxury fashion brands routinely commission high-craft collaborations, but few elevate the partner artisans into the campaign's centre of gravity rather than using them as decoration.
inferred.insight: True luxury craft is best honoured by matching the partner's level of handmade detail at every touchpoint of the campaign, not just on the product.
inferred.one_liner: Every campaign touchpoint, handmade like the ceramics.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Transformed a niche ceramicist collaboration into a multi-format craft universe — animated felted films, pop-up book, immersive pop-ups, 3D OOH — delivering 17.4M impressions and 1.6M engagements while broadening Loewe's reputation as the standard-bearer for artisanal luxury.

visual.style_description: Hero centre shows a hand-stitched needle-felted Suna Fujita doll-character clutching a leather Loewe handbag, surrounded by storybook-style felted ceramics scattered across the layout; the rest of the board is a soft grid of warm hand-crafted scenes (animated film stills, pop-up store interiors, packaging, OOH, book spreads) on a creamy off-white background.

idea.rationale: Rather than treating an artisan collaboration as product decoration, Loewe extended the partner's handmade craft into every campaign touchpoint, so the marketing itself became an artisan object. The needle-felted dolls, hand-crafted packaging landscapes and pop-up book translate Suna Fujita's ceramic vocabulary into a coherent storybook universe that is native to luxury and impossible to mass-replicate.

strategy.rationale: Loewe is LVMH's Madrid-founded leather house whose Jonathan Anderson era has been built on elevating obscure artisans (chairs, baskets, pottery) into the heart of luxury narrative, so partnering with an undiscovered Kyoto ceramicist couple is exact mission-fit, not a borrowed art collab. The strategic bet — that true luxury craft is honoured by matching the partner's level of detail at every touchpoint, not just on the product — is coherent with Loewe's craft-prize positioning. The board states the strategic premise as self-evident rather than backing it with audience research, which is the only soft spot.

execution.rationale: Orchestration spans bespoke needle-felted animated films, a long-form documentary on the ceramicist couple, hand-crafted landscape packaging, a custom children's pop-up book, 3D digital OOH where characters spring to life, and a candy-store-style immersive pop-up. Every channel inherits the same handmade material logic, so a digital billboard, a shipping box and an animated short all read as one continuous Suna Fujita world, which is rare for a global luxury rollout.

impact.rationale: 17.4M impressions and 1.6M engagements are entrant-reported reach figures that are credible but not independently audited, and the board does not disclose handbag sell-through, store traffic or brand-equity tracking — the business outcome a leather house normally cares about is conspicuously absent. The breadth of unpaid editorial across Vogue, Hypebeast, Elle, Vanity Fair, Forbes, Bazaar, Tatler and Grazia is a strong directional signal of fashion-press consensus, and the Grazia line 'nothing short of artistry' is the kind of category-defining quote that shifts brand equity beyond the campaign window.

why_it_won.verdict: Wins the Grand Prix because Loewe extended a niche ceramicist's handmade craft into every campaign touchpoint, so the marketing itself became the artisan object — a category-defining proof that luxury advertising can be as authored as the product.

one_liner: The luxury bag carries the anti-racism law inside.
search_doc:
The luxury bag carries the anti-racism law inside.
Mechanisms: everyday-object-transformation, legislative-advocacy, qr-as-reporting-tool, fashion-as-activism, cultural-symbol-subversion
Idea: In partnership with Brazil's most famous designers, we have launched a bag that prompts black consumers to exercise their legal rights; if when leaving a store, someone asks you to open it, they have a label displaying the law that protects racial insults to someone's dignity or honour based on color, and a QR code for reporting incidents to legal authorities on spot.
Insight: To raise awareness about the matter, Zumbi dos Palmares University decided to turn the symbol of discrimination into the very tool to fight it.

full_record.file_path: 2024/LUXURY & LIFESTYLE/SILVER/The Antiracist Bag - University Zumbi Dos Palmares (Grey, Sao Paulo).webp

extracted.tagline: The accessory that takes a stand against racism.
extracted.context: Over 50% of Brazil's population is Black. Still, Black Brazilians face discrimination everywhere, especially when shopping. Imagine having to open your bag every time you enter a store to prove you are not a thief?
extracted.insight: To raise awareness about the matter, Zumbi dos Palmares University decided to turn the symbol of discrimination into the very tool to fight it.
extracted.idea: In partnership with Brazil's most famous designers, we have launched a bag that prompts black consumers to exercise their legal rights; if when leaving a store, someone asks you to open it, they have a label displaying the law that protects racial insults to someone's dignity or honour based on color, and a QR code for reporting incidents to legal authorities on spot.
execution: Range of designer-made anti-racist bags, each carrying Law 14.532 on a label
QR code inside the bag opens a reporting platform connecting users to authorities
Bags designed by different leading Brazilian designers
+325k anti-racist agents in training
Partner ecosystem: Ambev, P&G, Cielo
Distribution of 'Tags distributed to enter your own bag'
press_coverage: By Boowy: "An unusual bag was created to fight racism in Brazil."
Estadão: "The bag exhibits a tag containing the law and a QR code to report."

inferred.one_liner: The luxury bag carries the anti-racism law inside.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turned the very accessory used to profile Black Brazilians into the tool to fight that profiling — designer bags carrying Brazil's anti-racism law and a QR reporting line trained +325K anti-racist agents, generated 43M impressions and a 16:1 return on investment.

visual.style_description: Editorial fashion-poster layout: a young Black woman in a forest-print designer dress holds up an open hunter-green tote whose interior label spells out Law 14.532 in yellow; a row of six designer anti-racist bags runs across the bottom; large mint-green 'Antiracist Bag' headline anchors the top, with neon callouts (+325K, 16:1, +43M) stacked on the right.

idea.rationale: Turning the exact object used to profile Black shoppers — the bag they are forced to open in stores — into the carrier of the law that protects them is a sharp cultural-symbol subversion. The everyday-object transformation is structurally clean: the racist demand to 'open your bag' now activates a reporting QR code instead of proving innocence.

strategy.rationale: Zumbi dos Palmares is a São Paulo university named after the 17th-century leader of the Palmares quilombo, with an explicit mission to train Black Brazilian leaders and fight structural racism, so weaponising a designer bag as a legal tool sits squarely within its advocacy remit. The context is data-anchored — over 50% of Brazil's population is Black, yet routine in-store profiling persists — and the bag carries the actual text of Law 14.532, which gives the activism legal specificity rather than slogan-level rhetoric. The luxury-category fit is the softest spot: the entrant is an activist university rather than a fashion house, but the designer-bag artefact is genuinely the medium, not a borrowed prop.

execution.rationale: Orchestration centres on a designer-bag range — each bag co-created with a different leading Brazilian fashion designer — carrying an internal label printing Law 14.532 and a QR code that routes incidents to legal authorities in real time. Partner ecosystem (Ambev, P&G, Cielo) provides distribution scale and the +325K 'anti-racist agents in training' wraps the product in an education layer. The board does not describe a phased rollout, retail-channel strategy or PR loop in detail, so the orchestration is competent but not richly mapped.

impact.rationale: +325K anti-racist agents in training is the right behaviour-change number for a university-led activism brief, though the board does not define what 'in training' counts as (completed module? signed up?), so the cohort definition remains soft. The 16:1 ROI is striking but unanchored — '16:1 against what investment base?' is not disclosed — and 43M impressions is entrant-reported reach rather than audited. Earned press from CNN, Estadão and By Boowy gives directional credibility, but the obvious cultural-shift outcome (in-store profiling reduction, complaint volume via the QR line) is absent from the board.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because turning the bag used to profile Black shoppers into the carrier of the law that protects them is a clean cultural-symbol subversion, but the impact case rests on unanchored percentages and a soft definition of 'agents in training'.

one_liner: Twiggy's 1967 Vogue shoot, restaged with eBay finds.
search_doc:
Twiggy's 1967 Vogue shoot, restaged with eBay finds.
Mechanisms: archive-as-campaign, celebrity-as-prop, magazine-as-media, vintage-as-product, sustainability-storytelling
Idea: We circled back over 150 years of Vogue archives and discovered that almost everything in the pages of Vogue could be sourced on eBay today. For Fashion Week, we invited Twiggy, icon from the 60s, back for the October issue of Vogue to recreate her famous shot from 1967 with the same vintage accessories found on eBay. The whole story was captured and turned into a Behind The Moment social video that was watched globally.
Insight: If iconic 1960s fashion still survives on eBay, fashion's sustainability problem is solvable by buying back the past instead of producing more of the future.

full_record.file_path: 2024/LUXURY & LIFESTYLE/BRONZE/Twiggy Full Circle - Ebay (Essencemediacom, New York).jpeg

extracted.tagline: To promote sustainability, we invited Twiggy back for the October issue of Vogue, her famous shoot from 1967, with the same vintage accessories found on eBay.
extracted.context: Fast Fashion contributes to 10% of CO2 emissions. To raise awareness of the toxic waste every year, eBay, home to millions of pre-owned and vintage fashion, wanted to promote sustainability.
extracted.idea: We circled back over 150 years of Vogue archives and discovered that almost everything in the pages of Vogue could be sourced on eBay today. For Fashion Week, we invited Twiggy, icon from the 60s, back for the October issue of Vogue to recreate her famous shot from 1967 with the same vintage accessories found on eBay. The whole story was captured and turned into a Behind The Moment social video that was watched globally.
execution: Vogue October issue cover and editorial reuniting Twiggy with her 1967 shoot accessories sourced on eBay
Behind-The-Moment social film of the shoot
Vogue and eBay collab on sustainability
Press accessories from the original 1967 Vogue shoot re-sourced on eBay platform
Cannes Planet Foundation partnership
press_coverage: Gotham: "Want to own a piece of fashion history? eBay and Vogue collab on vintage."
The Independent: "Flying the flag for sustainable fashion, Twiggy wears pre-loved fashion from eBay."

inferred.insight: If iconic 1960s fashion still survives on eBay, fashion's sustainability problem is solvable by buying back the past instead of producing more of the future.
inferred.one_liner: Twiggy's 1967 Vogue shoot, restaged with eBay finds.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Recreated Twiggy's 1967 Vogue cover using only vintage accessories sourced on eBay, generating $2.5M in earned media, +30% positive sentiment lift and +41% intent to shop pre-owned, with global pickup from NYT, BBC, CNN and Forbes.

visual.style_description: Editorial fashion-magazine spread layout: a recreated Vogue double-page hero in the centre showing Twiggy in red holding a Louis Vuitton trunk in two near-identical 1967-vs-2023 poses, with a small black-and-white inset of the original shoot to the left and a social Instagram comments column to the right; metric callouts on the right edge and a dense outlet logo footer.

idea.rationale: Restaging Twiggy's 1967 Vogue shoot inside the October Vogue using only vintage accessories sourced on eBay today is a clean archive-as-campaign reframe and a tidy proof that the past is buyable. The tonal precision is partial though: eBay is a mass-market resale marketplace rather than a native luxury house, and the work lands as a high-craft fashion editorial wrapped around a fundamentally non-luxury brand, which limits how far the idea can travel in this specific category.

strategy.rationale: eBay is the US-founded global secondhand marketplace whose 'recommerce' positioning leans on millions of pre-owned and vintage listings, so a sustainability brief is mission-coherent and the partnership with Vogue gives it credible fashion legitimacy. The context citing fast fashion as 10% of CO2 emissions is the right macro frame, but the board doesn't source the figure — and 10% is itself a debated, oft-contested figure in industry literature. The deeper Strategy tension is luxury-category fit: eBay is platform-economy resale, not heritage luxury, so the Vogue mounting is borrowed prestige rather than native maison heritage.

execution.rationale: Orchestration centres on a Vogue October cover and editorial reuniting Twiggy with the actual 1967 accessories re-sourced on eBay, then a Behind-The-Moment social film, a Cannes Planet Foundation partnership and a global press loop. The dual-image 1967-vs-2023 reveal is the right format for the idea and the print-into-social cascade is well sequenced for a Fashion Week window. Pairing eBay with Vogue lends the platform the editorial codes its category native lacks.

impact.rationale: $2.5M earned media is anchored as an absolute figure and credible for a Vogue-led press loop, with global pickup across NYT, BBC, CNN, Forbes, Reuters and Daily Mail giving real earned-conversation evidence. +30% positive sentiment and +41% intent to shop eBay pre-owned are entrant-reported brand-tracking lifts with no baseline disclosed — '+41% of which intent base, measured how?' is not specified — so they read as directional. The business outcome eBay would normally want (actual GMV in pre-owned fashion post-campaign) is absent, so impact rests on awareness and intent rather than transacted resale lift.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because re-sourcing Twiggy's 1967 Vogue accessories on eBay is a clean archive-as-campaign idea with strong earned reach, but eBay's mass-market identity sits awkwardly inside luxury and the brand-tracking lifts have no disclosed base.
